texts.orig: Long-range interactions assisted shortcuts to adiabaticity and battery charging in open quantum critical systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work we show that long-range interactions can be significantly beneficial for implementing shortcuts to adiabaticity (STA) in many-body open quantum critical systems driven out of equilibrium, as well as for charging quantum batteries in the presence of dissipation. In sharp contrast to short range interactions where passage through criticality may demand STA control with non-zero interactions between infinitely distant spins, using the example of a Kitaev chain with long-range couplings, we find that the corresponding control may involve involve interaction strength with decays algebraically with distance. In case of non-unitary control, the advantage of long-range interactions manifest through reduction in the cost of STA. We further propose a modified STA technique aimed at charging a quantum battery in the presence of dissipation, in which case long-range interactions may enhance the resultant ergotropy. Our results establish long-range interactions as a valuable resource for quantum control, with direct implications for quantum technologies.

In case of non-unitary control, the advantage of long-range interactions manifest through reduction in the cost of STA. We further propose a modified STA technique aimed at charging a quantum battery in the presence of dissipation, in which case long-range interactions may enhance the resultant ergotropy. Our results establish long-range interactions as a valuable resource for quantum control, with direct implications for quantum technologies.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Quantum systems driven out of equilibrium form a versatile area of research [1], owing to their fundamental nature, as well as their for potential applications in quantum technologies [2, 3] and condensed matter physics [4]. Recent progress in experimental research has resulted in significant advancements in our ability to control the dynamics of quantum systems for various tasks [5], including for preparation of desired quantum states [6, 7], for engineering novel phases of matter such as discrete [8] and continuous [9] time crystals, and for designing quantum technologies [10]. However, quantum systems driven out of equilibrium are in general associated with non-adiabatic excitations [11]. This can be detrimental, for example, for quantum annealing [12], or for modeling high-performing quantum technologies, including quantum computers [13] and quantum engines [14]. Consequently, studies on control in quantum systems, such as through shortcuts to adiabaticity (STA) [15], in order to reduce non-adiabatic excitations in driven quantum systems, are crucial for designing high-performing quantum technologies [14].
texts.text: Quantum systems driven out of equilibrium form a versatile area of research [1], owing to their fundamental nature, as well as their for potential applications in quantum technologies [2, 3] and condensed matter physics [4]. Recent progress in experimental research has resulted in significant advancements in our ability to control the dynamics of quantum systems for various tasks [5], including for preparation of desired quantum states [6, 7], for engineering novel phases of matter such as discrete [8] and continuous [9] time crystals, and for designing quantum technologies [10]. However, quantum systems driven out of equilibrium are in general associated with non-adiabatic excitations [11]. This can be detrimental, for example, for quantum annealing [12], or for modeling high-performing quantum technologies, including quantum computers [13] and quantum engines [14]. Consequently, studies on control in quantum systems, such as through shortcuts to adiabaticity (STA) [15], in order to reduce non-adiabatic excitations in driven quantum systems, are crucial for designing high-performing quantum technologies [14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: STA has been studied rigorously in closed quantum systems, where this technique may involve applying a control counterdiabatic (CD) Hamiltonian in order to ensure that the system remains in the instantaneous ground state of a time-dependent bare Hamiltonian at all times [16]. On the other hand, studies on STA in open quantum systems have received far less attention. Yet, their importance cannot be overstated, owing to the ubiquitous presence of dissipation in nature, and also due to the relevance of open quantum systems driven out of equilibrium in quantum condensed matter physics [17] and quantum technologies [18]. STA in open quantum systems can entail designing a control protocol such that the system follows a particular target eigenstate of the Liouvillian [19]. Alternatively, control can be desgined to ensure that the system remains in it's instantaneous thermal equilibrium corresponding to a particular temperature at all times, even in the presence of a time-dependent Hamiltonian [20]. As recently reported in Ref. [21], in contrast to STA in unitary dynamics where this protocol may involve many-body control terms only close to criticality [22], in the case of many-body open quantum systems control of entropy may result in the STA protocol involving many-body interacting control terms even away from criticality. Therefore rigorous studies on practically implementable STA protocols in many-body open quantum systems are crucial for designing near-term quantum technologies. To this end, in this work we study STA in a long-range Kitaev (LRK) model driven through quantum critical points, in the presence of a Fermionic bath. Our main motivation is to study whether the complexity of control can be controlled by tuning the range of interaction present in the bare Hamilotnian. Notably, we show that in sharp contrast to STA in the presence of nearest neighbor interactions where the unitary control terms may involve infinite-range interactions at the critical point [22], here long-range interactions (LRIs) result in a CD Hamiltonian involving interaction strength which decays algebraically with distance. The same can contribute to reduction of the cost of STA in the case of non-unitary control. We further use the protocol developed here to model a quantum battery in the presence of dissipation. Importantly, we show that depending on the inherent critical features of the model, LRIs may assist in enhancing the ergotropy of the quantum battery.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We now consider a quench across a quantum critical point in the presence of LRIs, with the interaction range controllable by a tuning parameter α . In general, one can expect the corresponding energy gap E ( α ) to be a function of the parameter α as well, such that E ( α ) → 0 close to criticality [29]. This in turn leads to non-adiabatic excitations in quantum critical systems driven out of equilibrium [11], thus necessitating the implementation of STA control protocols [22]. In general, the STA control terms can be expected to be functions of E ( α ) (see below, and also Refs. [16] and [20]), with the unitary control strength decreasing with increasing energy gaps [30]. Consequently, we expect setups where one can tune E ( α ) by varying α can be ideal for implementation of practically realizable STA protocols, through appropriate choices of the parameter α .

In general, the STA control terms can be expected to be functions of E ( α ) (see below, and also Refs. [16] and [20]), with the unitary control strength decreasing with increasing energy gaps [30]. Consequently, we expect setups where one can tune E ( α ) by varying α can be ideal for implementation of practically realizable STA protocols, through appropriate choices of the parameter α .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We exemplify the above using a detailed analysis of a LRK chain driven across quantum critical points. Importantly, a LRK chain is associated with two critical points with significantly different dispersion relations. In one case E ( α ) increases with increasing range of interactions (i.e., decreasing α ). As we discuss below, this allows us to implement STA with less demanding unitary control fields, through suitable choices of α . On the other hand, the other critical point is associated with E ( α ) increasing with decreasing range of interactions (i.e., increasing α ), which can negate the advantages associated with LRIs. In addition, we show that the above property of the LRK chain can also enable us to model a quantum battery with high ergotropy for longer ranges of interactions, through population inversion implemented by a modified STA protocol.

texts.orig: Here ˆ O m denotes the operators acting on sites separated by a distance m , while h m ( µ, α ) denotes the strength of the associated CD field. In the short-range limit α → ∞ , the pairing function reduces to f α ( k ) → sin k, and Eq. (17) reproduces the nearest-neighbor transversefield Ising model results derived in Ref. [22]. In that limit, the CD coefficients decay exponentially with distance, h m ( µ ) ∼ e -m/ξ ( µ ) with ξ ( µ ) being the correlation length of transverse-field Ising model. However, as we show below, the long-range structure of f α ( k ) may lead to a qualitatively different spatial structure of the CD field, especially near criticality.
texts.text: Here ˆ O m denotes the operators acting on sites separated by a distance m , while h m ( µ, α ) denotes the strength of the associated CD field. In the short-range limit α → ∞ , the pairing function reduces to f α ( k ) → sin k, and Eq. (17) reproduces the nearest-neighbor transversefield Ising model results derived in Ref. [22]. In that limit, the CD coefficients decay exponentially with distance, h m ( µ ) ∼ e -m/ξ ( µ ) with ξ ( µ ) being the correlation length of transverse-field Ising model. texts.orig: We plot the magnitude of the CD field h m (see Eq. (18)) close to the critical points µ = ± 1, as a function of the interaction exponent α , in Fig. 2. Notably, | h m | decreases as the system moves from the short-range regime ( α > 2) toward the long-range regime (1 < α < 2) for µ = -1. This stems from the enhancement of E k ( α ) with decrease in α for k → 0, thus showing the advantage of LRIs for suppressing non-adiabatic excitations in the this regime. On the other hand, the enhancement of E k ( α ) with increasing α for k → π results in the opposite trend being observed near the second critical point µ = +1, where short-range interactions require stronger control fields.

texts.orig: IV. STA IN THE PRESENCE OF DISSIPATION
texts.text: IV. STA IN THE PRESENCE OF DISSIPATION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section we investigate how LRIs modify the CD control associated with the dissipative dynamics. The system evolves according to the Lindblad master equation [Eq. (9)], where the dissipative STA protocol is implemented through time-dependent Lindblad operators A ( k ) mn ( t ) and the corresponding transition rates γ ( k ) mn ( t ). The explicit expressions for A ( k ) mn and γ ( k ) mn are summarized in Appendix C. The forms of γ ( k ) mn 's remain similar to that evaluated for the nearest neighbor interaction case in Ref. [21], albeit with the introduction of the parameter f α . As we discuss below, this in turn results in non-trivial dependence of the control terms on the range of interactions.

texts.orig: We now turn to the critical point µ = +1, where the gap closes linearly for k → π , ∀ α , both in high as well as low temperature regimes. Unlike the µ = -1 case, in this case the low-energy spectrum increases as the system crosses over from long-range to short-range interactions (i.e., with increasing α ). As a consequence, transition rates that scale as γ ( k ) mn ∼ 1 /E k ( α ) (eg. γ ( k ) 41 , γ ( k ) 31 = γ ( k ) 21 , γ ( k ) 23 = γ ( k ) 32 ) decrease with increasing α , thereby indicating the requirement of stronger rates of dissipative control for longer interaction ranges (see Fig.( 3)); the remaining γ 's become insensitive to α , as also seen for µ → -1. Furthermore, analogous to the µ = -1 case, high temperatures correspond to small values of γ ( k ) mn 's, owing to thermal mixing.

texts.orig: A. Heat Current
texts.text: A. Heat Current

texts.orig: VII. CONCLUSIONS
texts.text: VII. CONCLUSIONS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We studied shortcuts to adiabaticity (STA) in an open long-range quantum critical system, through suitable engineered unitary and non-unitary operators. We show that for suitable choices of parameters, LRIs can be significantly beneficial for controlling excitations in open quantum critical systems driven out of equilibrium, as well as for charging a quantum battery through a STA inspired quantum control technique.
texts.text: We studied shortcuts to adiabaticity (STA) in an open long-range quantum critical system, through suitable engineered unitary and non-unitary operators. We show that for suitable choices of parameters, LRIs can be significantly beneficial for controlling excitations in open quantum critical systems driven out of equilibrium, as well as for charging a quantum battery through a STA inspired quantum control technique.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work we have considered the LRK chain, which shows two different dispersion relations close to the two quantum critical points µ = ± 1; the energy gap E k ( α ) increases with increasing range of interactions for µ = -1, which in turn results in the long range advantages discussed above. In particular, for LRIs quantified by 1 < α < 2, the CD Hamiltonian strength h m denoting the strength of interactions between particles separated by a distance m decays algebraically with m both away from criticality, as well as at the criticality. This is in sharp contrast with the behavior seen for short range interactions obtained for α > 2, in which case h m assumes a m -independent constant value at criticality, analogous to that seen earlier for the transverse Ising chain in Ref. [22]. This result provides a clear way of harnessing LRIs for engineering practically realizable exact STA protocols in quantum critical systems, which otherwise can be expected to be impossible to implement, owing to the requirement of infinite-range interactions at criticality. In case of control in the presence of dissipation, LRIs assist in STA through reduction in the costs of control, quantified by decrease of magnitude of heat current with decreasing values of α . Finally we propose a modified STA protocol, which aims at using the setup as a quantum battery, through population inversion. We show that longer ranges of interactions are beneficial in this case as well, through enhancement of ergotropy for lower values of α , for µ ≈ -1.

texts.orig: The above discussed effects of LRIs and criticality become more pronounced for lower values of temperatures. On the other hand, thermal fluctuations dominate at high temperatures, thereby washing away all effects of α and criticality. Further, as discussed in Appendix E, inspite of the local form of the control Lindblad operators in the momentum space, the real space representation of the same involves multi-body interaction terms even away from criticality, for all values of α . This can be attributed to the requirement for control of entropy, which in general can be expected to involve non-local control terms.
texts.text: The above discussed effects of LRIs and criticality become more pronounced for lower values of temperatures. On the other hand, thermal fluctuations dominate at high temperatures, thereby washing away all effects of α and criticality. Further, as discussed in Appendix E, inspite of the local form of the control Lindblad operators in the momentum space, the real space representation of the same involves multi-body interaction terms even away from criticality, for all values of α . This can be attributed to the requirement for control of entropy, which in general can be expected to involve non-local control terms.

texts.orig: Finally, we note that the STA protocol presented in this work can be expected to be implementable in currently existing experimental platforms, including in ion traps [47] and quantum simulators [48, 49], and can be highly relevant for quantum control in many-body open quantum systems [50], as well as for applications in quantum technologies [51], including finite-time quantum engines [18] and quantum batteries [52].

(E5) can be expected to vanish in the limit of large D , as also verified numerically in Fig. 7, thus implying one can restrict D (12) 1 to small values only. Fig. (8) illustrates the behavior of the control strength J 12 as a function of the distance from criticality for different values of α , for a fixed D (= 1). As shown in Fig. (8), J 12 increases with increasing range of interactions (i.e., decreasing α ). Notably, the effect of α is suppressed close to criticality, which can be attributed to converging values of γ 12 for different values of α , at the critical points (see Figs. 3a, 3e, and Eq. (E2)). The insets of Fig. (8) show that, in the high-temperature regime, J 12 is significantly reduced, which is consistent with the fact that thermal mixing suppresses population gradients, thereby reducing the need for strong dissipative control.
